title: Design Software Cost Comparison for 2026
description: A comparison of design software pricing across Figma, Canva, Sketch, Framer, Webflow, and InVision — including free tiers, per-user costs, and what each plan actually includes.
body:
Design software pricing has stabilized into a pattern: freemium entry tiers that are genuinely useful for individuals, per-user monthly subscriptions that scale with team size, and enterprise plans that add administrative controls and security features. The gap between the free tier and the first paid tier varies dramatically across tools, and the value delivered at each tier determines whether a team is paying for capability or paying for access.

This comparison breaks down the published pricing for six design tools, examines what each tier includes, and identifies where the real cost surprises hide. All Figma pricing reflects publicly available rates verified in July 2026. Pricing for other tools is based on published rates at the time of writing — check each vendor's current pricing page for exact rates, as vendors change pricing periodically.

## Figma Pricing (Verified July 2026)

Figma operates on a per-editor subscription model with a generous free tier. The Free plan supports unlimited editors and up to three files, sufficient for individual designers or small teams evaluating the platform. The Professional plan at $16 per editor per month (annual billing; $20 per month if billed monthly) unlocks unlimited files, branch and merge, Dev Mode for developer handoff, and team libraries. The Organization plan at $55 per editor per month adds design system analytics, centralized administration, private plugins, and advanced branching. The Enterprise plan at $90 per editor per month includes SSO, SCIM provisioning, dedicated support, and advanced security controls.

Figma's pricing model means that team costs scale linearly with each additional editor. The upgrade from Professional to Organization is justified when the team needs design system analytics, centralized plugin management, or advanced branching workflows — capabilities that become essential as the design system matures.

The hidden cost in Figma is the transition from free to paid. Teams that start with the free tier and three files quickly outgrow it, and the jump to Professional represents a meaningful budget line item for startups with multiple designers.

## Canva Pricing

Canva's pricing structure targets a different buyer than Figma — marketing teams and non-designers who need visual content production rather than product design tooling. The Free tier provides access to thousands of templates, stock photos, and basic design tools. Paid tiers add brand kit functionality, background removal, a larger template library, and team collaboration features. Enterprise pricing is custom.

Canva's value proposition is speed over precision. A marketing team can produce social media graphics, presentations, and email headers in minutes using Canva templates, whereas the same deliverables in Figma would require custom design work. The tradeoff is design fidelity — Canva produces adequate visual content quickly, but it does not produce the pixel-perfect, component-based designs that product teams require.

Check Canva's current pricing page for exact rates on Pro and Teams tiers.

## Sketch Pricing

Sketch has transitioned from a one-time purchase model to a subscription. The Free tier provides basic editing with limited cloud collaboration. Paid tiers add the full editor, cloud collaboration, shared libraries, and centralized billing. Enterprise pricing is custom.

Sketch's pricing advantage is its lower per-user cost compared to Figma for Mac-only teams. The limitation is that Sketch's collaboration model requires higher tiers for team features, whereas Figma includes collaboration in the Professional tier.

The hidden cost in Sketch is the migration risk. As teams grow and hire designers on Windows or Linux, Sketch's Mac-only constraint forces a migration to Figma, incurring design file conversion costs and workflow disruption.

Check Sketch's current pricing page for exact rates on Standard and Business tiers.

## Framer Pricing

Framer's pricing reflects its positioning as a website builder rather than a design tool. The Free tier provides basic page building with Framer branding. Paid tiers remove branding and add custom domains, form submissions, custom code, and analytics. The pricing model is per-site rather than per-user, which creates a different cost structure than Figma or Sketch.

Framer's per-site model works well for marketing teams that need multiple landing pages but poorly for design teams that need collaborative editing across many files. Teams that build many pages accumulate costs quickly.

Check Framer's current pricing page for exact rates on Mini, Basic, and Pro tiers.

## Webflow Pricing

Webflow's pricing is structured around hosting capacity and site complexity. The Free tier provides basic site building with Webflow branding. Paid tiers remove branding and add custom domains, CMS hosting, form submissions, and API access. Enterprise pricing is custom.

Webflow's pricing reflects its dual identity as both a design tool and a hosting platform. Unlike Figma, which is purely a design tool, Webflow charges for both the design capability and the hosting infrastructure. Teams that use Webflow for design but deploy to external hosting still pay for the design capability.

Check Webflow's current pricing page for exact rates across Starter, Basic, CMS, and Business tiers.

## InVision Pricing

InVision's pricing has evolved significantly as the tool has shifted from a prototyping platform to a design review and collaboration tool. The Free tier provides basic prototyping and review for small teams. Paid tiers add advanced prototyping, unlimited boards, and priority support. Enterprise pricing is custom.

InVision's pricing advantage is its low per-user cost compared to Figma. For teams that primarily need design review and feedback workflows rather than collaborative design editing, InVision provides a purpose-built review platform at a competitive price point.

The limitation is that InVision's prototyping capabilities are significantly less sophisticated than Figma's. Teams that need interactive prototyping with conditional logic and state management will find InVision's capabilities insufficient, regardless of price.

Check InVision's current pricing page for exact rates on the Pro tier.

## Cost Comparison Summary

The lowest-cost option among these tools varies by team size and use case, but price alone is not the right selection criterion. The total cost of ownership — including the time spent on tooling, migration, and workflow friction — matters more than the subscription price.

A cheaper tool that requires workarounds or migration within two years costs more than an expensive tool that serves the team for five years. Teams should evaluate design tools based on the capabilities they need today and the scale they expect in two to three years, then select the tool that covers that trajectory at the lowest total cost.

The pricing model that matters most is not the per-user rate but the inflection points where costs jump. Understanding where free tiers end, where collaboration features unlock, and where enterprise requirements trigger upgrades helps teams budget accurately and avoid surprise costs.
author: PilotStack Team
